chunk_summary: This section explores pediatric chronic liver disease (CLD) nutrition pitfalls through case vignettes, emphasizing decision-making strategies for growth failure, ascites, and hepatic encephalopathy management.
learning_objectives: Understand the impact of excessive MCT use on essential fatty acid deficiency (EFAD) in cholestatic infants.
Develop strategies to manage ascites and hyponatremia in pediatric decompensated cirrhosis without compromising calorie delivery.
Learn the importance of restoring adequate protein intake in children with hepatic encephalopathy to support muscle mass and ammonia handling.
key_facts: Excessive MCT use can precipitate EFAD and worsen growth in cholestatic infants; mixed MCT/LCT strategies are recommended.
Hyponatremia in pediatric cirrhosis should be managed with time-limited fluid restriction, sodium restriction, and calorie-dense feeds.
Serum sodium levels of 124 mmol/L indicate severe hyponatremia requiring careful monitoring and intervention.
Prolonged protein avoidance in hepatic encephalopathy can lead to muscle loss; protein reduction should be brief and target levels restored promptly.
Fat-soluble vitamin monitoring is critical in cholestatic infants to prevent deficiencies during nutritional interventions.
reference_quote: Very high MCT reduces essential fatty acid delivery and can precipitate EFAD without improving calories. (NASPGHAN and ESPGHAN, 2019)
document_key_topics: Nutrition strategies in pediatric chronic liver disease
Interventions for ascites and hyponatremia in cirrhosis
Protein intake optimization in hepatic encephalopathy
